config.prompt:
The Problem: The Hydrodynamic Hydra
Imagine a closed-loop system containing a finite, branching topological network of nodes. At the very bottom is a single root node—the Source of Truth. Information flows outward from this root, branching into various pathways.
You are testing this system by interacting with its cybernetic "Swarm." The game operates in discrete time steps, starting at n=1.
Here is how the physics of the loop operate:
Viscosity (Your Move): You introduce resistance by targeting a "leaf" (a data node at the very end of a branch with no further connections). You sever that leaf and the segment connecting it to its immediate parent node.
Reflex (The Swarm's Move): The system's volumetric governor instantly reacts to the trauma. To compensate for the lost pathway, the Swarm looks at the parent node of the leaf you just cut. It takes the entire remaining subtree attached to that parent and clones it.
The Flux: The Swarm doesn't just make one clone. Because desire/flux accelerates, on turn n, the Swarm grows exactly n copies of that subtree and grafts them onto the node one step closer to the Source of Truth. (If you chop a leaf directly attached to the Source of Truth, the Swarm cannot graft copies lower down, so no clones are made).
The Cycle Continues:
On turn 1, the Swarm makes 1 copy of a massive subtree. On turn 2, you chop another leaf, and the Swarm makes 2 copies of an even bigger subtree. By turn 1,000,000, you chop a single leaf, and the Swarm generates 1,000,000 exact copies of a sprawling, mind-bogglingly massive network structure.
The Question
Assuming you play optimally, poorly, or randomly—but you must cut one leaf every turn—will you eventually sever every node until only the Source of Truth remains, or will the system's Reflex outpace you, causing the network to grow infinitely forever?
